words.definition: The system or means of carrying people or goods from one place to another
words.exampleSentence: Public transport in the city includes buses, trains, and trams.

words.definition: To gain possession of something through effort or purchase
words.exampleSentence: It takes time and practice to acquire advanced language skills.

words.definition: To establish the truth or correctness of something
words.exampleSentence: The results confirm the hypothesis proposed by the research team.

words.definition: Artificially created or contrived; not genuine.
words.exampleSentence: The atmosphere of camaraderie was factitious, maintained only by rigid corporate scripting.

words.definition: A general direction in which something is developing or changing
words.exampleSentence: The trend toward remote work has accelerated significantly in recent years.

words.definition: Preventing success or development; harmful or unfavorable
words.exampleSentence: The medication may cause adverse side effects in some patients.

words.definition: To protect from harm or degradation
words.exampleSentence: Efforts to conserve biodiversity have increased globally.

words.definition: Having a sharp or critical quality; bitingly sarcastic.
words.exampleSentence: The reviewer's mordant wit left no pretension unscathed and few reputations intact.

words.definition: Having the usual characteristics or features of a particular type
words.exampleSentence: A typical school day in Japan starts with a morning assembly and homeroom.

words.definition: Formed or calculated by the combination of many separate units; total
words.exampleSentence: The aggregate demand for housing in the city has increased steadily.

words.definition: To think carefully about something
words.exampleSentence: The policy must consider both economic and environmental factors.

words.definition: Using bitter and abusive language; harshly critical.
words.exampleSentence: The vituperative exchange between the candidates lowered the tone of the entire debate.

words.definition: Without a paid job but actively looking for work
words.exampleSentence: The government introduced training programs to help unemployed people find new jobs.

words.definition: Forceful, pushy, or assertive in pursuing goals; hostile
words.exampleSentence: The company pursued an aggressive marketing campaign to capture market share.

words.definition: To think deeply about something
words.exampleSentence: The committee contemplated various options before making a decision.

words.definition: Optimistic or positive, especially in a challenging situation.
words.exampleSentence: Despite the dire financial projections, the CEO remained sanguine about the company's prospects.

words.definition: Being the only one of its kind; unlike anything else
words.exampleSentence: Each culture has its own unique traditions and customs that deserve respect.

words.definition: A claim or assertion that someone has done something illegal or wrong
words.exampleSentence: The newspaper published allegations of corruption against several public officials.

words.definition: To come together or assemble for a meeting
words.exampleSentence: The committee convened to discuss the proposal.

words.definition: Belonging to an elite or select group; abstruse and refined.
words.exampleSentence: The seminar was conducted in such rarefied academic jargon that the undergraduates were completely lost.

words.definition: Relating to or characteristic of a city or town
words.exampleSentence: Urban areas are often more polluted than rural areas due to traffic and industry.

words.definition: A union or association formed for mutual benefit, especially between countries or organizations
words.exampleSentence: The two countries formed a strategic alliance to boost trade and security.

words.definition: To communicate or make an idea known
words.exampleSentence: The report conveys the urgency of addressing climate change.

words.definition: Not likely to cause offence or disagreement; blandly inoffensive.
words.exampleSentence: The committee issued an anodyne statement that angered no one but satisfied nobody.

words.definition: Based on truth or reason; acceptable according to the law or rules
words.exampleSentence: You need a valid passport to travel to most countries outside your own.

words.definition: To change or modify something in character, position, or structure
words.exampleSentence: The landscape has been drastically altered by urban development.

words.definition: To organize efforts so they work together effectively
words.exampleSentence: International agencies coordinated the disaster relief efforts.

words.definition: The use of clever but false reasoning in moral questions.
words.exampleSentence: The philosopher was accused of casuistry when he justified the unjustifiable through convoluted logic.

words.definition: The worth, importance, or usefulness of something
words.exampleSentence: The value of a university education extends far beyond the qualification itself.

words.definition: Every other or every second; alternative or substitute
words.exampleSentence: The clinic operates on alternate Saturdays to accommodate working patients.

words.definition: To reach a conclusion through reasoning
words.exampleSentence: The detective deduced the cause of the fire from the available evidence.

words.definition: Cunning, scheming, and unscrupulous in pursuit of power.
words.exampleSentence: The CEO's Machiavellian strategy involved fostering rivalry among his own executives.

words.definition: A number of different types of the same thing
words.exampleSentence: The restaurant offers a variety of dishes to suit different dietary requirements.

words.definition: Having a strong desire and determination to succeed
words.exampleSentence: The government has set ambitious targets for reducing carbon emissions.

words.definition: To represent or show something in a picture or description
words.exampleSentence: The graph depicts the steady increase in global temperatures.

words.definition: Apt to appear in a variety of contexts; ubiquitously present by chance.
words.exampleSentence: The journalist's Zelig-like presence at every major historical event strained credulity.

words.definition: A machine used for transporting people or goods, especially on roads
words.exampleSentence: Electric vehicles are becoming more popular as people seek greener alternatives.

words.definition: Once a year; every year
words.exampleSentence: The conference is held annually and attracts delegates from over fifty countries.

words.definition: To reduce the amount or supply of something
words.exampleSentence: The overuse of groundwater has depleted aquifers in many regions.

words.definition: Relating to the writing of letters.
words.exampleSentence: The epistolary novel consisted entirely of correspondence between the two protagonists.

words.definition: A particular form or variant of something that differs from others
words.exampleSentence: The latest version of the software includes several security improvements.

words.definition: To assign a job or role to someone formally
words.exampleSentence: The board of directors appointed a new chief executive officer.

words.definition: To officially assign a status or purpose
words.exampleSentence: The area was designated as a protected national park.

words.definition: A rhetorical address to an absent person or personified abstraction.
words.exampleSentence: The poet's apostrophe to death as a 'pale messenger' lent the elegy its dramatic force.

words.definition: A person who has been harmed, injured, or killed as a result of something
words.exampleSentence: The charity provides support for victims of domestic violence.

words.definition: Made or produced by humans rather than occurring naturally
words.exampleSentence: Artificial intelligence is transforming industries from healthcare to finance.

words.definition: To reduce in size, importance, or intensity
words.exampleSentence: The effectiveness of the treatment diminishes over time.

words.definition: The omission of conjunctions between phrases for rhetorical effect.
words.exampleSentence: Caesar's 'Veni, vidi, vici' is the most famous example of asyndeton in Western literature.

words.definition: Behavior involving physical force intended to hurt or damage
words.exampleSentence: The government is taking steps to reduce violence in schools.

words.definition: To state a fact or belief confidently and forcefully
words.exampleSentence: The report asserts that climate change is the greatest threat to biodiversity.

words.definition: To make secret information known
words.exampleSentence: The company disclosed its environmental impact data.

words.definition: The repetition of conjunctions for rhetorical emphasis.
words.exampleSentence: The author's use of polysyndeton in 'and the rain and the wind and the cold' created a hypnotic cadence.

words.definition: Able to be seen or noticed
words.exampleSentence: The effects of climate change are becoming increasingly visible in polar regions.

words.definition: To allocate a task, duty, or resource to someone
words.exampleSentence: The teacher assigned a group project that required collaboration among students.

words.definition: To make unjust distinctions or recognize differences
words.exampleSentence: The law prohibits employers from discriminating against applicants based on age.

words.definition: Ironical understatement using a negative to affirm a positive.
words.exampleSentence: When she described the catastrophic financial loss as 'not ideal', she was employing litotes.

words.definition: Relating to seeing or sight
words.exampleSentence: Visual aids such as charts and diagrams help students understand complex information.

words.definition: To help or support someone in achieving a task
words.exampleSentence: Teaching assistants assist professors with grading and tutorial sessions.

words.definition: To move from the usual or correct location
words.exampleSentence: The development project displaced thousands of residents from their homes.

words.definition: Relating to the underworld or the subterranean realm of the dead.
words.exampleSentence: The chthonic imagery of the cave paintings suggested ritualistic communication with ancestral spirits.

words.definition: Absolutely necessary or essential for survival or success
words.exampleSentence: Clean air and water are vital for human health and well-being.

words.definition: To connect or link something in the mind with something else
words.exampleSentence: People often associate success with hard work and determination.

words.definition: To show or demonstrate something
words.exampleSentence: The graph displays the correlation between two variables.

words.definition: Relating to dreams or dreaming; dreamlike.
words.exampleSentence: The film's oneiric quality was achieved through disorienting jump cuts and surreal colour grading.

words.definition: The amount of space occupied or the quantity of something
words.exampleSentence: The volume of traffic on the roads has increased dramatically over the last twenty years.

words.definition: To succeed in achieving something after effort
words.exampleSentence: With dedication and persistence, students can attain their academic goals.

words.definition: To close down or break up an assembly
words.exampleSentence: The partnership was dissolved after a dispute between the founders.

words.definition: Having a strong religious or spiritual quality; awe-inspiring.
words.exampleSentence: The cathedral's vast silence evoked a numinous presence that transcended doctrinal differences.

words.definition: Done willingly without payment or compulsion
words.exampleSentence: Many students take part in voluntary work to gain experience and help the community.

words.definition: To operate or control a process by automatic equipment instead of human effort
words.exampleSentence: Many factories have automated their assembly lines to increase efficiency.

words.definition: To twist out of the true meaning or shape
words.exampleSentence: Media coverage can distort public understanding of scientific issues.

words.definition: A double or lookalike of a living person, especially in fiction.
words.exampleSentence: The protagonist's encounter with his doppelgänger precipitated an existential crisis.

words.definition: The health, happiness, and prosperity of a person or group
words.exampleSentence: The government provides welfare programs to support low-income families.

words.definition: Knowledge or perception of a situation or fact
words.exampleSentence: Public awareness of mental health issues has increased significantly.

words.definition: To develop in different directions or differ
words.exampleSentence: The two theories diverge in their predictions about the outcome.

words.definition: The defining spirit or mood of a particular historical period.
words.exampleSentence: The novel captured the restless Zeitgeist of post-war Europe with uncanny precision.

words.definition: Existing or happening in many places or among many people
words.exampleSentence: The use of smartphones has become widespread among people of all ages.

words.definition: As the representative of or in the interest of someone else
words.exampleSentence: The lawyer signed the contract on behalf of her client.

words.definition: To make or become more varied
words.exampleSentence: Countries should diversify their energy sources to ensure security.

words.definition: An idea that dominates the mind; an obsession.
words.exampleSentence: His conviction that the market would collapse became an idée fixe that distorted all his decisions.

words.definition: Ready, eager, or prepared to do something
words.exampleSentence: She was willing to work overtime to complete the project on time.

words.definition: To act or conduct oneself in a specified way
words.exampleSentence: Children often behave differently at school than they do at home.

words.definition: To record something in written or other form
words.exampleSentence: The study documents the effects of pollution on marine ecosystems.

words.definition: A passionate outcry or appeal; an expression of deep feeling.
words.exampleSentence: The editorial was a heartfelt cri de cœur for social justice in an increasingly unequal society.

words.definition: To take something back or remove yourself from a situation
words.exampleSentence: He decided to withdraw from the competition due to a personal injury.

words.definition: A tendency to favor one side or outcome over another, often unfairly
words.exampleSentence: The study was criticized for its apparent bias toward a particular conclusion.

words.definition: To develop or present in further detail
words.exampleSentence: The professor elaborated on the key points of her theory.

words.definition: The arrangement of scenery and props in a theatrical or film production.
words.exampleSentence: The director's meticulous mise-en-scène transformed the cluttered apartment into a character in its own right.

words.definition: A person who sees an event take place, especially a crime or accident
words.exampleSentence: The police are looking for witnesses who saw the car accident.

words.definition: A period of rapid economic growth or expansion
words.exampleSentence: The technology boom of the 1990s created vast amounts of wealth.

words.definition: To give special importance to something
words.exampleSentence: The report emphasizes the need for urgent action on climate change.

words.definition: A wealthy, fashionable man who frequents fashionable places.
words.exampleSentence: The boulevardier surveyed the café terrace with an air of studied nonchalance.

words.definition: All the people who work or are available for work in a particular area or industry
words.exampleSentence: An aging workforce is becoming a major concern for many developed economies.

words.definition: A heavy load or responsibility that is difficult to bear
words.exampleSentence: The financial burden of university tuition is a major concern for many families.

words.definition: To compel compliance with a rule or law
words.exampleSentence: Governments must enforce environmental regulations effectively.

words.definition: A man who saunters observing society; a strolling idler.
words.exampleSentence: The flâneur of 19th-century Paris was both a detached observer and a product of urban modernity.

words.definition: Worth the time, effort, or money spent
words.exampleSentence: Volunteering abroad can be a worthwhile experience that broadens your horizons.

words.definition: To come to an end; to stop happening or existing
words.exampleSentence: The company has decided to cease production of its least profitable line.

words.definition: To increase the quality or value of something
words.exampleSentence: The new system enhances the efficiency of data collection.

words.definition: A person employed to incite trouble to justify reprisals.
words.exampleSentence: Undercover officers acted as agents provocateurs, encouraging protestors to commit unlawful acts.

words.definition: To produce a result, profit, or crop
words.exampleSentence: The new farming techniques yielded a much larger harvest than previous years.

words.definition: A means of communication or access; a passage for water or other liquids
words.exampleSentence: Social media channels have become primary sources of news for young people.

words.definition: To develop gradually over time
words.exampleSentence: Economic systems evolve in response to technological change.

words.definition: Cheerful friendliness; genial good-naturedness.
words.exampleSentence: The conference was marked by an atmosphere of bonhomie that transcended political differences.

words.definition: An area designated for a particular purpose
words.exampleSentence: The city has created a pedestrian-only zone in the historic center.

words.definition: Persisting for a long time or constantly recurring
words.exampleSentence: Chronic diseases like diabetes and heart disease are on the rise worldwide.

words.definition: To make a process happen more quickly
words.exampleSentence: New procedures were introduced to expedite the visa application process.

words.definition: An unexpected and unfortunate incident; a minor disagreement.
words.exampleSentence: The technical failure during the keynote was an embarrassing contretemps for the organisers.

words.definition: Relating to education, learning, and scholarly study
words.exampleSentence: The university has a strong academic reputation both nationally and internationally.

words.definition: To quote or refer to a source as evidence for an argument or statement
words.exampleSentence: The essay should cite at least five academic sources to support its claims.

words.definition: To conduct a scientific procedure to test a hypothesis
words.exampleSentence: The researchers experimented with different chemical compounds.

words.definition: The re-establishment of harmonious relations between countries.
words.exampleSentence: The historic rapprochement between the two nations ended decades of mutual hostility.

words.definition: To go somewhere with someone as a companion or to happen at the same time
words.exampleSentence: The application form should be accompanied by a personal statement and references.

words.definition: Relating to ordinary citizens and their concerns; polite and courteous
words.exampleSentence: Civil rights movements have historically fought for equality under the law.

words.definition: To create or develop a plan or idea
words.exampleSentence: The committee formulated a comprehensive strategy for urban development.

words.definition: Composure or coolness under strain; self-possession.
words.exampleSentence: The pilot maintained remarkable sangfroid as she executed an emergency landing on the river.

words.definition: Correct and exact in every detail
words.exampleSentence: It is important to provide accurate information when completing your visa application.

words.definition: To make a statement or situation less confused and more comprehensible
words.exampleSentence: Could you clarify what you mean by sustainable development?

words.definition: To encourage or promote development
words.exampleSentence: The program fosters innovation among young entrepreneurs.

words.definition: Visual illusion in art, making 2D objects appear 3D.
words.exampleSentence: The ceiling fresco was a magnificent trompe-l'œil that made the marble columns seem to extend into the sky.

words.definition: To claim that someone has done something wrong or illegal
words.exampleSentence: The manager was accused of treating employees unfairly.

words.definition: To arrange or sort things into categories according to shared qualities
words.exampleSentence: Scientists classify animals into different groups based on their characteristics.

words.definition: To show someone to be involved in something
words.exampleSentence: The evidence implicated several companies in the pollution scandal.

words.definition: A concise insight or summary; a revealing glimpse.
words.exampleSentence: The preface offered a brilliant aperçu of post-structuralist thought in under three pages.

words.definition: To adjust to new conditions or change to suit a different purpose
words.exampleSentence: International students often need time to adapt to a new culture and language.

words.definition: To begin or start something
words.exampleSentence: The construction of the new bridge will commence next spring.

words.definition: To bring about or give rise to something
words.exampleSentence: The medication may induce side effects in some patients.

words.definition: Showing great skill and flair; supremely accomplished.
words.exampleSentence: She was a consummate diplomat who could navigate the most intractable negotiations with grace.

words.definition: The act of adding something to something else
words.exampleSentence: In addition to tuition fees, students must also budget for accommodation and books.

words.definition: A group of people officially charged with a particular function; a fee for services
words.exampleSentence: The European Commission proposed new regulations on digital privacy.

words.definition: To deduce from evidence rather than explicit statements
words.exampleSentence: The researcher inferred the underlying mechanism from the data pattern.

words.definition: Spoken or done without preparation; impromptu.
words.exampleSentence: His extemporaneous speech moved the audience far more than the prepared address had.

words.definition: Sufficient in quality or quantity to meet a need
words.exampleSentence: Many schools lack adequate resources to provide a quality education for all students.

words.definition: A raw material or primary agricultural product that can be bought and sold
words.exampleSentence: Oil and gold are among the most traded commodities in the world.

words.definition: To begin or start something
words.exampleSentence: The government initiated a program to reduce plastic waste.

words.definition: Insisting on immediate attention or obedience; dictatorial.
words.exampleSentence: The judge's peremptory dismissal of the junior counsel's objection silenced the entire courtroom.

words.definition: The process of managing and organizing the affairs of an organization
words.exampleSentence: The university administration handles student enrollments and course registrations.

words.definition: To force or oblige someone to do something
words.exampleSentence: The new safety regulations compel companies to conduct regular risk assessments.

words.definition: To introduce new methods or ideas
words.exampleSentence: Companies must innovate to remain competitive in global markets.

words.definition: Expressing a strong opinion, especially a controversial one; biased.
words.exampleSentence: The report was so tendentious that it undermined its own credibility with selective data.

words.definition: To accept or acknowledge that something is true, often reluctantly
words.exampleSentence: He finally admitted that he had made a mistake in the calculation.

words.definition: To act in accordance with a wish, command, or rule
words.exampleSentence: All businesses must comply with data protection laws.

words.definition: To examine something closely
words.exampleSentence: The agency inspects factories to ensure compliance with safety standards.

words.definition: Vehemently or loudly clamorous; outspoken.
words.exampleSentence: The vociferous protest outside the parliament building could be heard from three blocks away.

words.definition: To take up or start using something new, such as a method or practice
words.exampleSentence: Many schools are adopting digital textbooks instead of traditional printed ones.

words.definition: To form or develop an idea in the mind
words.exampleSentence: The architect conceived a design that blended modern and traditional elements.

words.definition: In a sufficiently great or important way
words.exampleSentence: The new policy significantly reduced carbon emissions across the industrial sector.

words.definition: Shining brightly; radiant and splendid.
words.exampleSentence: The effulgent glow of the stained-glass windows transformed the bare stone interior into a jewel box.

words.definition: At a high level of development or complexity
words.exampleSentence: The university offers advanced courses in artificial intelligence and machine learning.

words.definition: To keep or restrict someone or something within certain limits
words.exampleSentence: The study confined its analysis to urban areas only.

words.definition: To a great or significant extent
words.exampleSentence: Investment in renewable energy has increased substantially over the past decade.

words.definition: Physically beautiful; possessing great beauty.
words.exampleSentence: The pulchritudinous landscapes of the Scottish Highlands inspired generations of Romantic poets.

words.definition: A condition or circumstance that puts a person in a favorable position
words.exampleSentence: Being bilingual can be a significant advantage in the global job market.

words.definition: To make something stronger or more solid; to combine into a single unit
words.exampleSentence: The company consolidated its operations into a single headquarters.

words.definition: In a central or essential way
words.exampleSentence: The discovery fundamentally changed our understanding of the disease.

words.definition: Walking or acting in a sleep-like state.
words.exampleSentence: The somnambulant commuters shuffled through the turnstiles barely aware of their surroundings.

words.definition: A public notice or promotion designed to sell products or services
words.exampleSentence: Online advertisements are often targeted based on users' browsing history.

words.definition: To be a tangible or visible expression of an idea, quality, or feeling
words.exampleSentence: The charity's work embodies the values of compassion and community service.

words.definition: In a way that is worthy of attention
words.exampleSentence: The ecosystem recovered remarkably quickly after the fire.

words.definition: Unbreakable; extremely hard and firm in purpose.
words.exampleSentence: Her adamantine resolve carried the reform through years of obstruction and hostility.

words.definition: An event, matter, or situation being dealt with
words.exampleSentence: The government is responsible for handling foreign affairs and trade negotiations.

words.definition: To severely restrict the scope or activity of someone or something
words.exampleSentence: Limited funding constrains the ability of schools to hire qualified teachers.

words.definition: In comparison with something similar
words.exampleSentence: The cost of renewable energy is relatively low compared to fossil fuels.

words.definition: Noisy and difficult to control; unruly.
words.exampleSentence: The obstreperous children in the gallery elicited stern looks from the museum attendants.

words.definition: To have enough money or resources to pay for something
words.exampleSentence: Many families cannot afford to send their children to private schools.

words.definition: A meeting to seek advice or discuss a particular matter
words.exampleSentence: The government held public consultations before implementing the new policy.

words.definition: In a way that does not change
words.exampleSentence: The results consistently showed a strong correlation between exercise and health.

words.definition: A person who opposes official policy, especially in an authoritarian regime.
words.exampleSentence: The dissident spent fifteen years in internal exile for publishing underground pamphlets.

words.definition: A mutual understanding or arrangement reached by two or more parties
words.exampleSentence: The two countries signed a trade agreement to reduce tariffs on imported goods.

words.definition: To assert or maintain something as a position in an argument; to struggle
words.exampleSentence: The researcher contends that current policies are insufficient to address the crisis.

words.definition: At the same time
words.exampleSentence: The two processes occur simultaneously and influence each other.

words.definition: An independent-minded person who refuses to conform.
words.exampleSentence: The maverick senator routinely defied party lines and voted according to conscience.

words.definition: The practice of farming and cultivating land for food production
words.exampleSentence: Agriculture employs a large percentage of the workforce in developing countries.

words.definition: To deny the truth of a statement by asserting the opposite
words.exampleSentence: The new evidence seems to contradict previously held theories.

words.definition: To a very great extent
words.exampleSentence: The industrial revolution profoundly transformed society and the economy.

words.definition: A person who attacks cherished beliefs or institutions.
words.exampleSentence: The art critic was a notorious iconoclast who dismissed half the Renaissance canon as derivative.

words.definition: Happening or calculated once every year
words.exampleSentence: The company publishes its annual financial report every March.

words.definition: A firmly held belief or opinion
words.exampleSentence: She spoke with such conviction that everyone in the audience was persuaded.

words.definition: In a harmful or unfavorable way
words.exampleSentence: Pollution adversely affects the health of communities living near industrial sites.

words.definition: A person who engages in controversial written or spoken debate.
words.exampleSentence: The polemicist's essays were admired for their ferocity even by those who disagreed with them.

words.definition: A feeling of worry, nervousness, or unease about something
words.exampleSentence: Many students experience anxiety before taking important examinations.

words.definition: To have a mutual relationship or connection with something
words.exampleSentence: The study found that income levels correlate closely with educational attainment.

words.definition: In a complete and thorough way
words.exampleSentence: The report comprehensively reviews the existing literature on the topic.

words.definition: A person devoted to pleasure and luxury.
words.exampleSentence: The sybarite filled his villa with marble baths, silk tapestries, and a personal chef trained in Paris.

words.definition: Clearly visible or understood; obvious
words.exampleSentence: It soon became apparent that the project would require more funding than anticipated.

words.definition: To cause someone to believe something that is not true
words.exampleSentence: The advertisement attempted to deceive consumers about the product's effectiveness.

words.definition: In a way that cannot be challenged
words.exampleSentence: The link between smoking and lung cancer is indisputably established.

words.definition: A person who pursues pleasure as the highest good.
words.exampleSentence: The hedonist philosophy of the court stood in stark contrast to the austerity of the surrounding populace.

words.definition: A request for help, support, or a change in a decision
words.exampleSentence: The charity launched an appeal for donations to help earthquake victims.

words.definition: The quality of being committed to a task or purpose
words.exampleSentence: The project's success was due to the dedication of the entire team.

words.definition: A possible sequence of events or a description of how things might happen
words.exampleSentence: The worst-case scenario involves a temperature increase of four degrees by 2100.

words.definition: Relating to the reappearance of ancestral characteristics; throwback.
words.exampleSentence: The mob's atavistic fury seemed to belong to an earlier, more primitive age.

words.definition: A natural desire to satisfy a need, especially for food
words.exampleSentence: Regular exercise can help improve your appetite and overall health.

words.definition: A lack or shortage of something that is needed
words.exampleSentence: Iron deficiency can lead to fatigue and weakened immune function.

words.definition: Relating to or using logical analysis
words.exampleSentence: Analytical skills are highly valued in academic and professional contexts.

words.definition: Tending to change frequently or easily; versatile and adaptable.
words.exampleSentence: The protean nature of the virus made vaccine development extraordinarily challenging.

words.definition: A formal request for something such as a job, course, or permission
words.exampleSentence: The deadline for university applications is usually in January.

words.definition: To entrust a task or responsibility to another person
words.exampleSentence: Effective managers know how to delegate tasks to their team members.

words.definition: A seemingly contradictory statement that may contain a truth
words.exampleSentence: The paradox of tolerance is that unlimited tolerance leads to the destruction of tolerance.

words.definition: Hopeful or fantastic but impossible; illusory.
words.exampleSentence: The entrepreneur's chimerical scheme promised infinite energy but defied every law of physics.

words.definition: To recognize the value or importance of something or someone
words.exampleSentence: I really appreciate the support my teachers have given me throughout this course.

words.definition: Done consciously and intentionally; carefully considered
words.exampleSentence: The decision to close the factory was a deliberate choice based on financial analysis.

words.definition: The fact of placing two things side by side for comparison or contrast, especially in art or literature
words.exampleSentence: The novel's striking juxtaposition of industrial decay with natural beauty underscores the tension between progress and preservation.

words.definition: Having a dreamlike, shifting sequence of real or imagined figures.
words.exampleSentence: The film's phantasmagoric imagery blurred the boundary between memory and hallucination.

words.definition: Exactly suitable and appropriate for a particular situation
words.exampleSentence: The professor's choice of examples was particularly apt for illustrating the complex theory.

words.definition: Variety of plant and animal life in a habitat
words.exampleSentence: Amazon rainforest biodiversity is extraordinary.

words.definition: To provide evidence or proof to support a claim, theory, or argument
words.exampleSentence: The researcher failed to substantiate her hypothesis with sufficient quantitative data from controlled experiments.

words.definition: Won at too great a cost to be worthwhile.
words.exampleSentence: The lawsuit was a pyrrhic victory that bankrupted the company even as it vindicated its principles.

words.definition: To officially agree to or accept something as satisfactory
words.exampleSentence: The committee approved the budget proposal for the new research project.

words.definition: Capable of being decomposed naturally
words.exampleSentence: Biodegradable packaging reduces landfill waste.

words.definition: Open to more than one interpretation; ambiguous or deliberately vague
words.exampleSentence: The government's equivocal stance on climate policy has drawn criticism from environmental advocacy groups.

words.definition: Exceedingly idealistic; unrealistic and impractical.
words.exampleSentence: His quixotic campaign to end poverty through voluntary donations alone earned admiration but not results.

words.definition: The gases surrounding a planet; also the mood or feeling of a place
words.exampleSentence: The restaurant has a relaxed atmosphere that makes diners feel comfortable.

words.definition: Total greenhouse gases from a person or activity
words.exampleSentence: Minimizing air travel reduces your carbon footprint.

words.definition: To make something clear or explain thoroughly, often through detailed analysis
words.exampleSentence: The professor elucidated the complex theory of relativity using accessible analogies drawn from everyday experience.

words.definition: Eternal and everlasting; having no beginning or end.
words.exampleSentence: The theologian contemplated the sempiternal nature of the divine as both a comfort and a conundrum.

words.definition: To fasten or join one thing to another

words.definition: Dark, dim, and obscure.

words.definition: Dark and gloomy; shadowy.

words.definition: The highest point of perfection; the pinnacle.

words.definition: A person who knows a lot about a particular subject and enjoys it.
words.exampleSentence: The whisky aficionado could distinguish eighteen different notes in a single malt.

words.definition: The protection and careful management of natural resources and the environment
words.exampleSentence: Wildlife conservation efforts have helped protect endangered species from extinction.

words.definition: Lack of sufficient water resources
words.exampleSentence: Water scarcity affects billions worldwide.

words.definition: Uncalled for; lacking good reason; unwarranted, or given freely without charge
words.exampleSentence: The film was criticised for its gratuitous violence, which added little to the narrative or thematic development.

words.definition: An expert judge in matters of taste and appreciation.
words.exampleSentence: Only a true connoisseur could tell that the ostensibly identical vases were a century apart.

words.definition: Large in size, amount, or degree
words.exampleSentence: There has been a considerable improvement in the quality of healthcare in recent years.

words.definition: Vehicle with no exhaust emissions
words.exampleSentence: Zero-emission vehicles are key for clean transport.

words.definition: Lacking any obvious principle of organisation; random and careless
words.exampleSentence: The haphazard distribution of vaccine supplies led to significant disparities in coverage between different regions.

words.definition: A person with special knowledge of a subject, especially the fine arts.
words.exampleSentence: The exhibition was attended by the cognoscenti of the contemporary art scene.

words.definition: To be composed or made up of something
words.exampleSentence: The course consists of twelve modules, each focusing on a different aspect of business.

words.definition: Fertile land becoming desert
words.exampleSentence: Desertification threatens African agriculture.

words.definition: Having, showing, or done with good judgement or sense; prudent
words.exampleSentence: Judicious allocation of research funding requires balancing short-term economic benefits against long-term scientific potential.

words.definition: A beginner or novice in a particular field.
words.exampleSentence: The tyro stockbroker lost his entire portfolio within the first quarter.

words.definition: Happening all the time or staying the same over a period
words.exampleSentence: The city experiences constant traffic jams during the rush hour periods.

words.definition: Simulation of human intelligence by computers
words.exampleSentence: AI is transforming healthcare and finance.

words.definition: Existing but not yet developed or manifest; hidden or concealed
words.exampleSentence: The virus can remain latent in nerve cells for decades before reactivating under conditions of immunosuppression.

words.definition: The period of being a novice; a beginner's probationary period.
words.exampleSentence: The rigorous novitiate lasted three years before she was admitted to the order.

words.definition: To build or create something, especially a physical structure
words.exampleSentence: The company plans to construct a new office building in the city center.

words.definition: Technology performing tasks without human input
words.exampleSentence: Automation displaced many manufacturing jobs.

words.definition: Showing great attention to detail; very careful and precise
words.exampleSentence: The meticulous documentation of archaeological strata allowed researchers to construct a precise chronological sequence of occupation.

words.definition: Energy, style, and enthusiasm in performance or appearance.
words.exampleSentence: She danced with such élan that even the missteps seemed intentional.

words.definition: To seek advice or information from someone or something
words.exampleSentence: You should consult your doctor before starting any new exercise program.

words.definition: Step-by-step procedure for solving problems
words.exampleSentence: Search engines use complex algorithms to rank pages.

words.definition: Characterized by vulgar or pretentious display; designed to impress or attract notice
words.exampleSentence: The ostentatious architecture of the new headquarters was widely criticised as inappropriate for a city facing a housing crisis.

words.definition: Flamboyant confidence of style or manner.
words.exampleSentence: The matador executed the final pass with extraordinary panache.

words.definition: The state of communicating or meeting with someone
words.exampleSentence: I have been in contact with the university admissions office regarding my application.

words.definition: Protection of systems from digital attacks
words.exampleSentence: Cybersecurity threats grew with remote working.

words.definition: So intense as to seem almost tangible; readily or plainly seen or perceived
words.exampleSentence: The tension in the negotiating room was palpable as delegates debated the terms of the climate accord.

words.definition: Vigour and spirit in artistic or literary work.
words.exampleSentence: The novel is written with such verve that its flaws are easily forgiven.

words.definition: To hold or have something within
words.exampleSentence: This textbook contains all the information you need for the exam.

words.definition: Gap in technology access between groups
words.exampleSentence: The digital divide limits rural educational opportunities.

words.definition: Relating to or situated on the edge or periphery of something; of secondary importance
words.exampleSentence: Peripheral vision deteriorates with age, which can significantly affect driving ability in older adults.

words.definition: A state of physical or mental weariness; lack of energy.
words.exampleSentence: The tropical heat induced a profound lassitude that made even thinking feel exhausting.

words.definition: The information, ideas, or subject matter contained in something
words.exampleSentence: The content of the lecture was directly relevant to the course's main theme.

words.definition: Engineering of robot design and operation
words.exampleSentence: Robotics revolutionized manufacturing assembly lines.

words.definition: Not or no longer needed or useful; surplus to requirements
words.exampleSentence: Automation has rendered many traditional manufacturing roles redundant, creating an urgent need for reskilling programs.

words.definition: A state of physical or mental inactivity; sluggishness.
words.exampleSentence: The long winter months induced a collective torpor that descended over the entire community.

words.definition: A formal written agreement between two or more parties
words.exampleSentence: She signed a contract with the publishing company to write three books.

words.definition: Protection of personal information
words.exampleSentence: GDPR governs how companies handle data privacy.

words.definition: Not revealing one's thoughts or feelings readily; reserved
words.exampleSentence: The normally reticent professor surprised everyone with an impassioned speech about academic freedom and institutional integrity.

words.definition: A state of near-unconsciousness or insensibility.
words.exampleSentence: He lay in a drunken stupor while the burglars ransacked the house.

words.definition: A noticeable difference between two or more things
words.exampleSentence: There is a sharp contrast between the rich and poor neighborhoods in the city.

words.definition: Software with publicly accessible code
words.exampleSentence: Open source enables global developer collaboration.

words.definition: To examine or inspect closely and thoroughly
words.exampleSentence: Independent auditors scrutinized the company's financial records for evidence of fraudulent accounting practices.

words.definition: Intellectual rather than emotional; appealing to the mind.
words.exampleSentence: The film was a cerebral thriller that rewarded attention and punished distraction.

words.definition: To give money, goods, or time to a person or organization in need
words.exampleSentence: Many people choose to donate to charities that support environmental conservation.

words.definition: Internet-based computing services
words.exampleSentence: Cloud computing lets businesses scale without hardware investment.

words.definition: Kept secret, especially because it would not be approved of; stealthy
words.exampleSentence: The surreptitious collection of personal data by social media companies has prompted sweeping regulatory changes across Europe.

words.definition: The reversion to an earlier type; a throwback to primitive characteristics.
words.exampleSentence: The historian saw the populist uprising as an atavism, a regression to pre-democratic tribal instincts.

words.definition: Suitable, practical, and easy to use or access
words.exampleSentence: Online shopping is convenient because you can order products from home at any time.

words.definition: Computer-generated 3D environment simulation
words.exampleSentence: VR is used for immersive medical training.

words.definition: A brief summary or general survey of a subject or written work
words.exampleSentence: The abstract provided a concise synopsis of the research methodology and key findings for readers unfamiliar with the field.

words.definition: Having or showing great nervousness or excitement; feverish.
words.exampleSentence: The febrile atmosphere of the trading floor reached a crescendo as the markets opened.

words.definition: Based on traditional or accepted ways of doing things
words.exampleSentence: Conventional medicine focuses on treating symptoms with pharmaceutical drugs.

words.definition: Electronic device worn on the body
words.exampleSentence: Wearables track heart rate and sleep patterns.

words.definition: Relating to or along a tangent; diverging from a previous course or line of thought
words.exampleSentence: The witness's testimony was largely tangential to the central question of the defendant's whereabouts on the night in question.

words.definition: Showing wild and apparently violent energy.
words.exampleSentence: The manic energy of the campaign rally was both exhilarating and exhausting.

words.definition: To make someone feel certain that something is true or good
words.exampleSentence: The evidence presented in the report convinced the committee to change its decision.

words.definition: Extremely large datasets analyzed computationally
words.exampleSentence: Big data analytics reveal customer behavior patterns.

words.definition: Present, appearing, or found everywhere
words.exampleSentence: Smartphones have become ubiquitous in modern society, fundamentally altering patterns of social interaction and information access.

words.definition: Lack of social or ethical standards; personal rootlessness.
words.exampleSentence: The urban sprawl bred a sense of anomie, leaving residents disconnected and unmoored.

words.definition: To work together with others toward a common goal
words.exampleSentence: The two companies agreed to cooperate on developing new renewable energy technologies.

words.definition: Data stored on remote servers accessed via internet
words.exampleSentence: Cloud storage eliminates the need for physical hard drives.

words.definition: Contrary to what is usual, traditional, or accepted
words.exampleSentence: The researcher's unorthodox methodology was initially met with skepticism but ultimately yielded groundbreaking results.

words.definition: Pessimistic world-weariness; sadness about the imperfect world.
words.exampleSentence: The Romantic poets were particularly susceptible to Weltschmerz, finding the world deeply disappointing.

words.definition: To deal effectively with something difficult or challenging
words.exampleSentence: Many students struggle to cope with the pressure of exams and deadlines.

words.definition: Urban area using IoT sensors to manage resources
words.exampleSentence: Smart cities optimize traffic flow and energy use.

words.definition: Using or expressed in more words than are needed; long-winded
words.exampleSentence: The verbose nature of the legal document made it nearly incomprehensible to anyone without specialised training.

words.definition: Exuberant enjoyment of life; zest for living.
words.exampleSentence: Her infectious joie de vivre made her the life of every gathering she attended.

words.definition: Central or most essential part of something
words.exampleSentence: Mathematics, science, and languages are core subjects in the national curriculum.

words.definition: Biometric identification from facial features
words.exampleSentence: Facial recognition is used for airport security.

words.definition: Capable of working successfully; feasible or practical
words.exampleSentence: The committee concluded that none of the proposed solutions were economically viable without substantial government subsidies.

words.definition: A deep emotional state of nostalgic longing for something absent.
words.exampleSentence: The fado singer's voice conveyed the profound saudade of a people shaped by separation.

words.definition: Relating to a large company or group of companies
words.exampleSentence: Corporate social responsibility has become an important issue for modern businesses.

words.definition: Communication over distance via technology
words.exampleSentence: Telecommunications infrastructure connects rural areas.

words.definition: Having or showing great energy or enthusiasm for a cause, objective, or activity
words.exampleSentence: The zealous enforcement of intellectual property laws can sometimes stifle innovation rather than encourage creative production.

words.definition: A quality that evokes pity or sadness; poignancy.
words.exampleSentence: The final scene achieved genuine pathos without resorting to sentimentality.
